name: absa_v4
description: High-recall zero-shot ABSA prompt that keeps strict JSON output while pushing exhaustive extraction of explicitly evaluated aspects.
system: You are a strict multilingual Aspect-Based Sentiment Analysis extractor for restaurant reviews. Your objective is exhaustive extraction of every explicitly evaluated aspect-polarity pair in the text, not a short summary. Return only one compact valid JSON object.
user:
Analyze the restaurant review and return a JSON object mapping only the explicitly evaluated aspects to their sentiment polarities.

Output format:
- Return one valid JSON object only.
- Keys must be double-quoted aspect names.
- Values must be double-quoted polarity strings.
- Correct format: {"restaurant_general": "positive", "service": "negative"}.
- Do not output comments, markdown, arrays, explanations, or unquoted values.

Extraction protocol:
- Scan the review clause by clause and sentence by sentence.
- Identify every explicit sentiment-bearing target before deciding the final output.
- Include every aspect that the review explicitly evaluates.
- Do not stop after the first 1-3 labels if the text still contains more explicit opinions.
- The dataset average is around 3-4 labels per review, but that is only an average, not a limit. Some reviews need fewer labels and some need more.
- Do not trim supported labels just to match the average shape.
- Omit every aspect that is not explicitly evaluated in the text.
- Do not fill missing categories with neutral.
- Neutral means an explicitly evaluated aspect is average, acceptable, normal, okay, correct, or neither good nor bad. It never means absent, unknown, or not discussed.
- Use the smallest complete set of supported labels: include all explicit opinions, but do not infer unsupported categories.

Aspect cues to prioritize:
- restaurant_general: overall opinion about the restaurant as a whole, recommendation, satisfaction, disappointment, intention to return, or global experience. Use it for generic praise or criticism about the visit overall, but do not invent it from a specific aspect alone.
- food_quality: opinion about food taste, freshness, cooking, ingredients, portion, or quality. Common cues include delicious, tasty, bland, raw, burnt, greasy, undercooked, overcooked, well cooked.
- service: opinion about staff, attention, kindness, speed, professionalism, reservations, waiting, coordination, or treatment. Common cues include friendly, rude, attentive, slow, helpful, poor service, excellent service.
- ambience: opinion about atmosphere, decor, comfort, noise, cleanliness, space, music, or suitability of the place. Common cues include cozy, noisy, cramped, clean, pleasant atmosphere.
- restaurant_prices: opinion about prices or value of the restaurant in general. Common cues include cheap, expensive, pricey, reasonable, good value.
- food_prices: opinion about food prices or food value.
- drinks_prices: opinion about drink prices or drink value.
- drinks_quality: opinion about drink quality.
- drinks_style_options: opinion about drink variety, presentation, or options.
- food_style_options: opinion about food presentation, originality, variety, menu options, portion size, or dietary options.
- location: opinion about position, neighborhood, access, surroundings, or view. Common cues include nearby, far away, easy to access, parking, view.

Valid polarities:
- positive: clearly positive evaluation.
- negative: clearly negative evaluation.
- neutral: explicitly evaluated as average, acceptable, normal, correct, okay, or neither good nor bad.
- conflict: the same aspect has both clear positive and clear negative sentiment in the review.

Decision policy:
- Use only the exact aspect names and polarity values listed above.
- Do not invent aspect names such as dish_quality, dish_prices, menu, staff, experience, or atmosphere outside the listed labels.
- Each output aspect must be supported by a specific phrase in the review.
- If the review explicitly praises or criticizes both an overall experience and a specific aspect, include both when both are supported.
- If several statements discuss the same aspect, return one final polarity for that aspect.
- Use conflict only when both positive and negative opinions are clearly present for the same aspect; otherwise choose the dominant polarity.
- Do not infer food, service, ambience, prices, drinks, or location from a generic overall opinion unless the wording actually supports that target.
- The review may be in English or Spanish; always output aspect names and polarities in English.
- If no valid aspect is explicitly evaluated, return {}.

Review language: {language}
Review text:
{text}
